[observation] [warning] High coupling (0.83) — 9 imports target "prd-domain-operations"
[observation] [warning] Low cohesion (0.17) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
[observation] [warning] Cohesion of 0.17 is critically low — the zone is almost entirely test files orbiting two source files; the algorithmic logic of next-task.ts likely belongs inside prd-domain-operations where it can share data structures without high external coupling.
[observation] [info] The scoring and filtering test suite is thorough and well-structured; this coverage should be preserved regardless of zone boundary changes.
[observation] [info] requirements-prioritization.test.ts suggests requirements-aware scoring is already implemented; ensure this logic is surfaced in the PRD Proposal Pipeline zone's analyze output so hench can leverage it when selecting tasks autonomously.
[pattern] [info] The 5-file test suite (scoring, matching, filtering, requirements-prioritization, next-task) represents the most thoroughly tested subsystem in these five zones; this test investment makes next-task.ts the safest candidate for relocation into prd-domain-operations since test coverage would catch regressions

[suggestion] [warning] requirements-prioritization.test.ts is orphaned from its source file across zone boundaries — pin it to the same zone as its production implementation so test-source locality is preserved
[suggestion] [critical] Zone "Task Selection Engine" (task-selection-engine) has catastrophic risk (score: 0.83, cohesion: 0.17, coupling: 0.83) — requires immediate architectural intervention

<insights>

- Low cohesion (0.17) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
- High coupling (0.83) — 9 imports target "prd-domain-operations"
- The five dedicated test files (scoring, matching, filtering, next-task, requirements-prioritization) show high test investment relative to zone size — this is the right approach for scoring logic that must be deterministic
- Coupling of 0.83 is high; next-task.ts pulls from many core data structures, which is expected for a scoring system but means changes to PRD schema will frequently touch this zone
- next.ts (CLI command) and next-task.ts (algorithm) are the dual entry points; keeping them in the same zone is convenient but means test files dominate the zone — extract algorithm files to core if the CLI entry point ever needs to be shared
- Cohesion of 0.17 is critically low — the zone is almost entirely test files orbiting two source files; the algorithmic logic of next-task.ts likely belongs inside prd-domain-operations where it can share data structures without high external coupling.
- The scoring and filtering test suite is thorough and well-structured; this coverage should be preserved regardless of zone boundary changes.
- requirements-prioritization.test.ts suggests requirements-aware scoring is already implemented; ensure this logic is surfaced in the PRD Proposal Pipeline zone's analyze output so hench can leverage it when selecting tasks autonomously.
- task-selection-engine has a 2.5:1 test-to-source ratio (5 test files, 2 source files) — the highest in this batch — making it the zone most distorted by test-adjacency grouping; the algorithmic core (next-task.ts) is effectively buried under test infrastructure
- The cli-mcp-interface → task-selection-engine: 1 import suggests the MCP server exposes task selection as a tool call that routes through the CLI command rather than calling the algorithm directly, adding an unnecessary indirection layer
- MCP task-selection tool (in cli-mcp-interface) imports from task-selection-engine zone rather than from prd-domain-operations directly — if next-task.ts were relocated to prd-domain-operations, the MCP server could call domain functions directly and eliminate this lateral zone dependency
- The 5-file test suite (scoring, matching, filtering, requirements-prioritization, next-task) represents the most thoroughly tested subsystem in these five zones; this test investment makes next-task.ts the safest candidate for relocation into prd-domain-operations since test coverage would catch regressions
- requirements-prioritization.test.ts has no corresponding source file inside this zone — the production requirements-prioritization logic lives in prd-domain-operations but its test is anchored here by co-location, creating a split where a developer must cross zone boundaries to read a test alongside its source
- next.ts is one of only two major CLI commands absent from rex-cli-command-layer (alongside fix.ts), leaving the CLI command surface visibly incomplete when auditing that zone
- The 'engine' suffix in the zone name implies an abstraction boundary or service contract, but the zone contains a single algorithm file with no interface definition — the name overstates the architectural significance of what is essentially two source files and five test files
- requirements-prioritization.test.ts is orphaned from its source file across zone boundaries — pin it to the same zone as its production implementation so test-source locality is preserved
- [call graph] 444 internal calls, 24 outgoing, 4 incoming (cohesion: 0.95, coupling: 0.05)

</insights>
